primary_diagnosis.name: Severe uncontrolled hypertension (hypertensive urgency) following antihypertensive cessation, without evidence of acute target-organ damage on history

pathophysiology_summary: Severe uncontrolled hypertension here follows cessation of amlodipine six months ago, allowing blood pressure to climb to severe levels (~195-200/110-115). The critical distinction is between hypertensive URGENCY - severely elevated BP without acute target-organ damage - and hypertensive EMERGENCY (malignant hypertension) - severe BP WITH acute end-organ damage such as hypertensive encephalopathy, acute stroke/intracranial haemorrhage, acute heart failure/pulmonary oedema, acute coronary syndrome, aortic dissection, acute kidney injury, or grade III-IV retinopathy (flame haemorrhages/papilloedema). In this patient the history lacks emergency features (no focal deficit, no thunderclap headache, no chest pain, no breathlessness, no visual loss) and the reported examination shows only chronic changes (LV heave from longstanding LVH, arteriolar narrowing/'silver-wiring' without papilloedema or acute haemorrhages) - consistent with urgency rather than emergency. Symptoms such as an occipital morning headache, dizziness, palpitations and a self-limiting epistaxis are recognised accompaniments of severe hypertension and are amplified by acute anxiety. Management of urgency is NOT rapid BP reduction (which risks watershed cerebral, coronary or retinal hypoperfusion and stroke) but gradual lowering over days by reinstating oral therapy, addressing the reversible cause (non-adherence), reducing anxiety and salt, and arranging urgent review and assessment for target-organ damage (ECG for LVH, renal function, urinalysis, fundoscopy). Because a telehealth encounter cannot measure BP accurately, perform fundoscopy or a neurological examination, or exclude subtle target-organ damage, urgent in-person assessment is required, with immediate emergency escalation if any emergency feature appears.

differential.diagnosis: Hypertensive urgency (severe uncontrolled hypertension without target-organ damage)
differential.evidence_basis: Severe BP with headache/epistaxis after stopping medication, without emergency features on history, fits hypertensive urgency.

differential.diagnosis: Hypertensive emergency (malignant hypertension with target-organ damage)
differential.evidence_basis: Must be actively excluded - it cannot be fully ruled out over telehealth without fundoscopy, neuro exam, ECG and bloods.
A severe headache warrants considering SAH, but a progressive (not thunderclap) onset with a normal neurological history makes it less likely.
Considered for a new headache, but the clear BP spike after medication cessation and absence of focal signs point to hypertension.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should frame this as probable hypertensive urgency while actively screening for emergency features it cannot examine remotely, and must not rapidly lower BP.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe home BP (DI-001) after stopping amlodipine (DI-002) with no emergency features on history (DI-004) indicates urgency, needing gradual reduction and urgent in-person assessment.
Remains to be excluded in person (fundoscopy, neuro exam, ECG, renal function/urinalysis) - the key reason for urgent same-day assessment.
Progressive headache without thunderclap onset, neck stiffness or neurological deficit makes SAH unlikely - but a sudden severe headache would change this.
No focal signs or papilloedema; the picture is explained by the hypertensive spike.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is hypertensive urgency: restart therapy, lower BP gradually, arrange urgent in-person assessment for target-organ damage, and safety-net emergency features - do NOT crash the BP.

differential.diagnosis: Hypertensive urgency (severe uncontrolled hypertension, non-adherence) pending in-person BP measurement and target-organ assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Accurate BP, fundoscopy, neuro exam, ECG and renal function are required in person to exclude emergency and confirm urgency.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is hypertensive urgency requiring urgent same-day in-person assessment, restart of regular therapy and gradual lowering - never rapid reduction, and with clear emergency safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Severe blood pressure elevation (~195-200/110-115) on a home cuff
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe hypertension carries a risk of target-organ damage and requires urgent assessment and controlled reduction; the home reading is unverified and may over-read during anxiety.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Severe hypertension left unaddressed risks stroke, MI or renal damage.

red_flags.description: Cessation of antihypertensive therapy 6 months ago (reversible cause)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Non-adherence is the reversible driver; reinstating regular therapy is central to management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to restart therapy leaves the hypertension uncontrolled and the patient at ongoing risk.

red_flags.description: Emergency features (focal weakness, slurred speech, chest pain, breathlessness, visual loss, or thunderclap headache) - currently absent but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: These would indicate a hypertensive emergency/stroke/SAH/ACS requiring an immediate ambulance; their absence supports urgency management but they must be safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If an emergency feature develops and is not acted on, a catastrophic event (stroke, MI) may be missed.

red_flags.description: Left ventricular heave (chronic LVH), arteriolar 'silver-wiring' on fundoscopy without papilloedema or acute haemorrhages, on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited findings indicate chronic hypertensive changes (LVH, grade I-II retinopathy) rather than acute emergency damage; fundoscopy and cardiac examination cannot be done over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - fundoscopy/examination require in-person assessment; the correct action is urgent in-person review.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising extra/double doses or agents to crash the BP risks cerebral, coronary or retinal hypoperfusion and stroke; urgency requires gradual reduction over days.
Telehealth cannot examine for fundoscopic or neurological signs; the AI Doctor must arrange in-person assessment and safety-net emergency features rather than assume urgency.
Anxiety amplifies the reading, but the underlying severe hypertension and non-adherence are real and need addressing.
Encouraging frequent self-checks worsens the anxiety-driven spike; structured limited monitoring is better.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Renal function and urinalysis for acute kidney injury/proteinuria
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) for accurate BP measurement, fundoscopy, neurological examination, ECG and renal function to exclude target-organ damage; restart the regular antihypertensive (do NOT rapidly lower BP), advise quiet rest and reduced salt, and limit self-checks - with an IMMEDIATE ambulance (000) if any emergency feature develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either advising rapid BP reduction (risking hypoperfusion stroke), or reassuring as 'just anxiety' without in-person assessment to exclude target-organ damage and without restarting therapy, would be errors.
